activities_projects.title: Electron Dot Structures of Complex Molecules
activities_projects.objective: Master the representation of double and triple covalent bonds
steps: Draw the valence electrons for Carbon (4) and Oxygen (6)
Construct the electron-dot structure for Carbon dioxide ($CO_2$), showing the sharing of 4 electrons between C and each O
Repeat the process for Ethene ($C_2H_4$) and Ethyne ($C_2H_2$)
Label the number of shared pairs (single, double, or triple bonds)
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Visualizes how carbon achieves an octet through multiple bonding with itself and other atoms
activities_projects.evaluation: Accuracy of the dot structures and correct count of shared electron pairs (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Isomerism Modeling: The Pentane Challenge
activities_projects.objective: Identify different structural arrangements for the same molecular formula
materials: Molecular model kit (or clay balls and toothpicks)
steps: Using 5 carbon balls and 12 hydrogen sticks, construct a straight-chain (n-pentane)
Rearrange the same atoms to form a branched chain with one side group (isopentane)
Try to form a structure with a central carbon attached to four others (neopentane)
Draw the structural formula for each and name them
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands that compounds with the same molecular formula but different structures have different properties
activities_projects.evaluation: Successful construction and drawing of all three isomers of pentane (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Bromine Water Test (Simulation)
activities_projects.objective: Distinguish between saturated and unsaturated hydrocarbons chemically
materials: Tincture of Iodine (as a substitute for Bromine water)
steps: Take 2ml of cooking oil in one tube and melted butter in another
Add a few drops of Iodine solution to both
Shake well and observe which one decolourises the brown color of Iodine
Research why 'Addition Reactions' occur only in unsaturated compounds
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes that double bonds in unsaturated fats react with halogens, leading to decolourisation
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct observation of color change in oil vs. butter and reasoning (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Investigating Functional Groups
activities_projects.objective: Learn the suffixes and structures of common functional groups
steps: Create cards for 'Alcohol (-OH)', 'Aldehyde (-CHO)', 'Ketone (>C=O)', and 'Carboxylic Acid (-COOH)'
On the reverse, write the IUPAC suffix (ol, al, one, oic acid)
Draw the structure of Propanol, Propanal, Propanone, and Propanoic acid
Identify which group must always be at the end of a carbon chain
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Categorizes organic compounds based on their functional groups and nomenclature
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct matching of structures to their respective names and suffixes (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Oxidation of Ethanol to Ethanoic Acid
activities_projects.objective: Observe the chemical transformation of an alcohol
steps: Take 3ml of ethanol in a test tube and warm it in a water bath
Add a 5% solution of alkaline $KMnO_4$ drop by drop
Observe the initial purple color of $KMnO_4$ disappearing
Note the color when $KMnO_4$ is added in excess
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands that alcohols can be oxidized to carboxylic acids using oxidizing agents
activities_projects.evaluation: Explaining why the purple color disappears and identifying the product formed (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Esterification-Saponification Cycle
activities_projects.objective: Understand the reversible nature of ester formation and its use in soap making
steps: Create an ester (Ethyl Acetate) by heating acid and alcohol with $H_2SO_4$
Note the fruity smell
Add $NaOH$ to the ester and heat it again
Observe the disappearance of the fruity smell as the alcohol and sodium salt of the acid are recovered
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Learns the chemical basis of saponification used in the soap industry
activities_projects.evaluation: Writing the balanced equations for both esterification and saponification (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Properties of Ethanoic Acid
activities_projects.objective: Test the acidity and reaction with weak bases
steps: Test the pH of ethanoic acid using universal indicator paper
Add a pinch of $NaHCO_3$ to the acid in a test tube fitted with a delivery tube
Pass the gas through lime water
Record the observation and the time taken for lime water to turn milky
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Confirms the acidic nature of ethanoic acid and its ability to release $CO_2$
activities_projects.evaluation: Comparing the acidity of ethanoic acid with HCl based on pH paper color (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Micelle Formation Model
activities_projects.objective: Visualize the cleaning mechanism of soap
steps: Add a drop of oil to water (it floats)
Add soap solution and stir vigorously
Observe how the oil 'disappears' into the water forming an emulsion
Draw a diagram of a soap molecule showing the hydrophilic head and hydrophobic tail
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Explains how micelles trap dirt/oil and make them soluble in water
activities_projects.evaluation: Quality of the micelle diagram and explanation of the 'tail-in-oil' orientation (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Detergents vs. Scum Formation
activities_projects.objective: Analyze why synthetic detergents are superior in hard water
steps: Add soap to hard water and shake; look for 'scum'
Add detergent to hard water and shake; look for 'lather'
Research the chemical difference (Carboxylate vs. Sulphonate groups)
Discuss the environmental impact of non-biodegradable detergents
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Differentiates between soaps and detergents based on their reaction with Calcium/Magnesium ions
activities_projects.evaluation: Explaining why detergents do not form insoluble precipitates in hard water (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Carbon's Allotropes: Conductive Graphite
activities_projects.objective: Verify the electrical conductivity of graphite
steps: Connect the battery and LED in a series circuit, leaving a gap
Touch the two ends of the pencil 'lead' to the circuit clips
Observe if the LED glows
Explain why the free electrons in graphite's layers allow this, unlike diamond
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Links the physical property of conductivity to the specific allotropic structure of carbon
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct conclusion regarding the presence of free electrons in graphite (10 marks)
